content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Supply-side contraction of the selective track (shrink the gate, not the rules)'. Early selection at age 10–12 into the 6- and 8-year grammar school tracks is justifiable only if Hungary can sustain politically durable institutional design, ensure between-school equity gains translate into individual-level SES equalization, and maintain implementation continuity across electoral cycles—outcomes Poland achieved partially and lost entirely, demonstrating that the benefits of delayed selection (1 SD gain for at-risk students, improved labor-market outcomes, reduced school segregation) outweigh the benefits of early tracking only if political reversibility risk and individual-SES gains (not achieved in Poland) are weighted equally with test-score gains.
opportunities: Shrinks the selective track's footprint without requiring statutory redefinition of the secondary-education age boundary, potentially avoiding the felmenő-rendszer and ~1-year lead-time obligations that a structural reform would trigger.

Aligns policy with the demographic reality that the exam's original rationing function is eroding, as evidenced by falling 8-year-track applications (-14% year-on-year) alongside flat overall enrolment.
strengths: Poland's 1999 delayed-tracking reform, which extended comprehensive general education from 8 to 9 years (moving selection from age 12 to age 15), produced PISA score gains of 0.25 SD in mathematics, 0.28 SD in reading, and 0.16 SD in science between 2000 and 2006; students in likely-vocational tracks gained approximately one standard deviation (~100 PISA points).
Between-school equity improved markedly after the 1999 reform: Poland reduced performance disparities between schools from one of the largest in the OECD to one of the smallest.
threats: Reversal requires legislative repeal since score thresholds are maintainer-level administrative practice rather than statute, offering more procedural friction than S1's decree-based mechanism, but a parliamentary majority change during the multi-year rollout still poses a significant reversal risk given the Fidesz framing of any selection-related change and the government's own pattern of rapidly reversing predecessor reforms.

Church and foundation maintainers, protected by Tisza's coalition commitment to preserve prior operating conditions, may simply expand to absorb displaced demand, neutralizing the intended contraction and creating a policy-credibility problem.
weaknesses: The precise causal decomposition between the 1999 reform's structural effect (unified intake) versus its resource/composition effects (investment and teacher change) remains uncertain; even Jakubowski et al.'s two-thirds/one-third split is an estimate and could shift with different methodologies.

Whether individual-level SES gaps could have been closed by longer-duration exposure to the 1999 reform (the Polish cohort was measured at age 15, not adulthood) or whether structural timing reform alone is insufficient without targeted SES-based support programs remains untested.
